coredata.dc:title: Interaction between fluorescein isothiocyanate and carbon dots: Inner filter effect and fluorescence resonance energy transfer
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Huabing
Xu, Chaoyong
Bai, Yanli
Liu, Lin
Liao, Dongmei
Liang, Jiangong
Liu, Lingzhi
Han, Heyou
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have been widely used for the preparation of multifunctional probes by conjugation with organic fluorescent dyes. However, the effect of organic fluorescent dyes on CDs still remains poorly understood. Herein, the effect of fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) on CDs was explored by spectroscopic techniques at pH5.1, 7.0 and 9.0. The fluorescent intensity of CDs was found to be quenched gradually after mixing directly with different concentrations of FITC, but the fluorescent lifetime of CDs remained unchanged. According to the results of UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescent lifetime measurements, a pH-dependent inner filter effect (IFE) between CDs and FITC was proposed. However, the fluorescent lifetime of CDs deceased after their conjugation with FITC, implying the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between CDs and FITC. This study has revealed two different effects of FITC on CDs with varying pH values and provided useful theoretical guidelines for further research on the interaction between other nanoparticles and fluorophores.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescein isothiocyanate
Carbon dots
Inner filter effect
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Investigation of the pH-dependent effect of FITC on CDs
Effect of FITC on the fluorescence of CDs
The pH-dependent IFE of FITC on CDs
The optical property of CDs-FITC conjugates.
The occurrence of FRET in CDs-FITC conjugates

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The UV–vis absorption, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the as-prepared CDs at the concentration of 76.6μg/mL. Inset: images of CDs under irradiation of sunlight (left) and UV (right, 365nm) light.

Effect of FITC on the fluorescence spectra of CDs in phosphate buffer solution with different pH values: (a) 5.1, (b) 7.0 and (c) 9.0. The concentration of CDs was fixed at 0.31mg/mL, and the concentrations of FITC were 0, 0.71, 2.12, 3.54, 4.96, 7.08, 10.60, 14.20 and 21.20μM separately.
The normalized fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs and FITC.
The fluorescence decay curves of CDs in the absence and presence of FITC under excitation of 370nm.
The actual process of fluorescence measurement in this study.
The UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, FITC, CDs-FITC mixtures and the sum value of absorbance of CDs and FITC at different pH values: (a) 5.1, (b) 7.0 and (c) 9.0.
The dependence of I 0/I on the concentrations of FITC within the range of 0–21.20μM at different pH values.
Fluorescence emission spectra of (a) CDs and (b) FITC in the pH range of 5.1 to 9.0, (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of FITC in the pH range of 5.1 to 9.0.

Normalized spectral overlaps (marked by shadow) between the emission spectra (solid blue line) of CDs and the UV–vis absorption spectra (dashed red line) of absorber FITC in the pH range of 5.1 to 9.0.
The fluorescence decay curves of CDs and CDs-FITC conjugates upon excitation of 370nm.
Fluorescence lifetimes of CDs, CDs-FITC mixtures and CDs-FITC conjugates in phosphate buffer solution with different pH values.
The efficiency of IFE between CDs and FITC at different pH values.

head.ce:title: Interaction between fluorescein isothiocyanate and carbon dots: Inner filter effect and fluorescence resonance energy transfer

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, State Key Laboratory of Agricultural Microbiology, Huazhong Agricultural University, Wuhan 430070, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Agricultural Microbiology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have been widely used for the preparation of multifunctional probes by conjugation with organic fluorescent dyes. However, the effect of organic fluorescent dyes on CDs still remains poorly understood. Herein, the effect of fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) on CDs was explored by spectroscopic techniques at pH5.1, 7.0 and 9.0. The fluorescent intensity of CDs was found to be quenched gradually after mixing directly with different concentrations of FITC, but the fluorescent lifetime of CDs remained unchanged. According to the results of UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescent lifetime measurements, a pH-dependent inner filter effect (IFE) between CDs and FITC was proposed. However, the fluorescent lifetime of CDs deceased after their conjugation with FITC, implying the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between CDs and FITC. This study has revealed two different effects of FITC on CDs with varying pH values and provided useful theoretical guidelines for further research on the interaction between other nanoparticles and fluorophores.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past several decades, the interaction between nanoparticles and fluorophores or other chemicals has attracted widespread attention among researchers, which has facilitated the development of various fluorescence nanosensors. For example, Wu et al. [1] have designed a dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent probe for detection of Zn2+ based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between different-colored quantum dots and meso-tetra(4-sulfonatophenyl)porphine dihydrochloride (TSPP). Yan et al. [2] have developed a sensitive ratiometric fluorescent sensor for detection of organic phosphorus pesticides based on inner filter effect (IFE) between gold nanoparticles and quantum dots. Xu et al. [3] have established a new gold nanoparticles-based sensor for detection of thiols due to FRET between gold nanoparticles and BODIPY. These reports suggest the importance and necessity of the research on the interaction between nanoparticles and fluorophores. However, most current studies are mainly focused on quantum dots [4–7], metal nanoparticles [8–10], metal nanoclusters [11–13], and organic fluorescent dyes [14–16].

Carbon dots (CDs), a new type of fluorescent nanoparticles, have aroused strong interest among researchers due to their unique characteristics and distinct optical properties [17–21], such as good water solubility, satisfactory environmental friendliness, favorable photostability, facile surface functionalization, high biocompatibility and excellent cell membrane permeability. These attractive features indicate the prominent advantages of CDs in chemical sensing [22–25], biosensing [2,26,27], bioimaging [28–31], nanomedicine [32], and catalysis [33,34]. Recently, a series of CDs-based systems have been constructed, especially the pH sensors based on CDs and fluorescent dyes that are designed to accurately quantify the pH values in cells and even in organelles [35,36]. Generally, in the process of designing a pH sensor, the most commonly used fluorescent dye is FITC [37–41], which can be easily conjugated with a variety of nanoparticles through amino group, and the nanoparticles can still maintain their optical properties and quantum effect after conjugation with FITC.

To date, the studies on CDs and organic fluorescent dyes have mainly focused on their applications in chemical and biological fields, and little information is available on the interactions between CDs and organic fluorescent dyes such as FITC. To our best knowledge, the effect of FITC on CDs has not been studied systematically, indicating the necessity of the research on the effect of organic fluorescent dyes such as FITC on CDs.

Herein, the effect of FITC on CDs was studied systematically. Due to the high sensitivity of FITC to pH, the experiment was conducted in different pH values, and the effect of FITC on CDs was explored in two different ways: mixing and conjugation.

Fluorescence measurements were performed on a RF-5301PC spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu, Japan) equipped with a 20kW xenon discharge lamp as a light source. The UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained on a UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) with a 1.0cm path-length quartz cuvette. Fluorescence lifetime measurements were recorded on an FLS920 spectrometer from Edinburgh Instruments Ltd. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were collected on a Nicolet Avatar-330 spectrometer (Thermo, USA) using the KBr pellet technique. Zeta potential and hydrodynamic diameters were measured with a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 DLS system (Malvern, England). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were acquired by a JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan) operating at an acceleration voltage of 200kV. A PHS-3C pH-meter (INESA, China) was used for pH measurements.

4,7,10-Trioxa-1,13-tridecanediamine (TTDDA, 96%) and fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC, 95%) were purchased from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co. Ltd. Citric acid anhydrous (99.5%) and quinine sulfate dihydrate (99.0%) were obtained from Aladdin Regent Co. Ltd. Glycerin, ninhydrin and other common solvents or salts were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Regent Co. Ltd. Ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.2MΩ·cm was used throughout the experiments. All these reagents were of analytical grade or better and used as obtained.

The amino-functionalized CDs used in this study were synthesized as previously reported [35]. Briefly, 15.00mL glycerin was mixed with 1.00mL TTDDA and heated to 220°C under nitrogen atmosphere. Subsequently, 1.0054g citric acid anhydrous was added and the temperature was kept at 220°C for 3h. The obtained dark brown turbid mixture was purified via exhaustive dialysis to remove the impurities and centrifuged to remove the precipitate. The supernatant was collected and stored in dark at 4°C for further use. The concentration of the as-prepared CDs solution was determined to be 3.83mg/mL.

CDs-FITC conjugates were prepared using a method analogous to others [35]. Briefly, 5.00mL of the amino-functionalized CDs solution prepared above (3.83mg/mL) was mixed with 15.00mL of FITC (177μM in water) in 0.10M NaHCO3 (pH=8.31). After reaction at room temperature for 36h, the resulting labeled CDs solution was purified via thorough dialysis using a dialysis membrane with a molecular weight cut-off of 1000Da against 0.10M NaHCO3 for 24h and ultrapure water for another 24h (48h in total) to remove the unbound FITC. The conjugates were then stored at 4°C as the stock solution for subsequent spectral experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the pH-dependent effect of FITC on CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical experiment to test the pH-dependent effect of FITC on CDs, different volumes of 177μM FITC in water were added to 0.31mg/mL CDs solutions in 20mM phosphate buffer solution in the pH range of 5.1 to 9.0. All the solutions were subjected to fluorescence (excitation wavelength of 370nm) and UV–vis absorption spectral measurement immediately after mixing.

To explore the mechanism of the effect of FITC on CDs, the fluorescence lifetimes of CDs in CDs-FITC mixtures in the pH range of 5.1 to 9.0 were measured at wavelength of 450nm under the excitation of 370nm upon mixing. A similar procedure was adopted for the lifetime measurement of CDs-FITC conjugates.

The morphology and size of the as-prepared CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis. TEM image (Fig. S1a) showed that the synthesized CDs were well dispersed and uniform in shape. DLS measurement (Fig. S1b) revealed that the particles had good size distribution in the range of 2–6nm with an average size of 3.7nm. The functional groups on the surface of CDs were identified by FT-IR spectra. As shown in Fig. S1c, CDs exhibited five characteristic absorption bands centered at 1112cm−1, 1404cm−1, 1701cm−1, 2873cm−1 and 3421cm−1, which can be ascribed to the stretching vibration of CO, CN, CO, CH and OH, respectively. To further prove the existence of amino group, a Kaiser test was conducted. As shown in Fig. S2, the solution of CDs turned an intense blue color after the Kaiser test with ninhydrin, and the UV–vis absorption spectra changed greatly. The results indicated that the prepared CDs were modified with amino group, which was beneficial for further surface functionalization of CDs.

Fig. 1 depicts the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence properties of the as-prepared CDs. It can be clearly seen that the maximum absorption of CDs was located at 370nm with the optimal fluorescence emission centered at 450nm. Under the irradiation of UV lamp (365nm), strong blue fluorescence from CDs was observed. To investigate the detailed fluorescence properties of CDs, a series of emission spectra were recorded in the excitation wavelength range of 340 to 390nm. With the excitation wavelength increased from 340 to 390nm (Fig. S3), the emission peak showed a slight red shift, indicating the good quality of CDs and the reduction in surface defects. The quantum yield (QY) of CDs was calculated to be 6.5% according to a previously reported procedure [24,30], using quinine sulfate as standard (QY=54%). Furthermore, the obtained CDs stock solution can maintain a long-term (several months) homogeneous phase without any visible precipitation at 4°C, suggesting the stability of the particles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of FITC on the fluorescence of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the interaction between CDs and FITC, different amounts of FITC were added into a fixed concentration of CDs solution. Since FITC can conjugate to amino-functionalized CDs, fluorescence measurements were conducted immediately after mixing. From Fig. 2a, it can be clearly seen that the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased gradually with increasing concentration of FITC at pH5.1, indicating an fluorescence quenching of CDs by FITC. The same results were also obtained when FITC was mixed with CDs at pH7.0 (Fig. 2b) and pH9.0 (Fig. 2c).

Fluorescence lifetime measurement can effectively distinguish quenching mechanism. The fluorescence emission spectrum and UV–vis absorption spectrum of FITC are shown in Fig. 3 . Considering the large spectral overlap of the absorption spectrum of FITC with the emission spectrum of CDs, the quenching of CDs by FITC may be induced by FRET or IFE. To discriminate the quenching mechanism, fluorescence lifetime measurements were carried out for CDs in the absence and presence of FITC at varied pH values. As shown in Fig. 4 and Table 1 , with the addition of FITC, the average fluorescence lifetime of CDs remained unchanged, indicating the absence of FRET between CDs and FITC when mixed directly. Additionally, no significant variation was observed in the fluorescence lifetimes of CDs under varied pH values, indicating the good stability of CDs towards pH.

Considering the spectral overlap between CDs and FITC, the observed fluorescence quenching of CDs by FITC may have resulted from IFE. Before exploring the question of whether the fluorescence quenching of CDs was caused by IFE, the fluorescence spectra should be corrected first. Recently, a simplified correction factor based on mathematical model of IFE has been deduced and widely used to calculate the deviation of IFE [42–45]. The result can be expressed by the following equation:
(1)
I corr
I obsd
=
10
A ex
x 1
+
A em
y 1

Here, x1 and y1 represent the optical path length of excitation and emission beams while A ex and A em denote the absorbance per centimeter at the excitation and emission wavelengths, respectively. In this paper, the fluorescence measurements were conducted as shown in Fig. 5 , and the optical path length of excitation and emission beams were determined to be a/2 and b/2, respectively. Thus, the corrected fluorescence intensity can be expressed by:
(2)
I corr
=
I obsd
⋅
10
A ex
a +
A em
b
/ 2

The UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs-FITC mixtures are shown in Fig. S4. From the absorbance at the excitation wavelength and emission wavelength, the corrected fluorescence intensities of CDs were obtained. After accurate calculation and analysis, it was found that the corrected fluorescence intensities of CDs almost remained unchanged with increasing FITC concentration. Therefore, it can be concluded that the fluorescence decrease of CDs by FITC upon mixing was mainly attributed to IFE rather than static quenching. Under the excitation of 370nm, CDs are excited and relax to ground state by emitting fluorescence. Due to the absorption of FITC at the emission wavelength of CDs, the emitted light will be absorbed by FITC in the mixture, leading to the occurrence of IFE. To further exclude the presence of static quenching, the absorption spectra of CDs, FITC and CDs-FITC mixtures were examined. When FITC was added, no significant difference was observed between the absorption spectra of CDs-FITC mixtures and those of the sum value of CDs and FITC (Fig. 6 ). The results indicated that no CDs-FITC complex was formed and the fluorescence decrease of CDs mainly resulted from IFE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The pH-dependent IFE of FITC on CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
It is worth noting that the observed fluorescence decrease degree of CDs varied in different pH values (Fig. 2). In order to quantify the fluorescence decrease degree of CDs, the efficiency of IFE was calculated via a slightly modified Stern-Volmer equation, which was usually used to calculate the fluorescence quenching caused by IFE [46,47].
(3)
I 0
I
= 1 +
K IFF
Q

Similar to the Stern-Volmer equation, I 0 represents the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the absence of FITC and I represents the fluorescence intensity of CDs under different concentrations of FITC. K IFF is the efficiency of IFE and [Q] is the concentration of FITC. Since K IFF can be reflected by the slope of the linear equation, the dependence of I 0/I on the concentrations of FITC at varied pH values was discussed. As shown in Fig. 7 , the slope of the plots increased with decreasing pH. The efficiency of IFE under the acidic condition was found to be obviously higher than that at pH7.0 and 9.0 (Table 2 ), suggesting a pH-dependent fluorescence decrease of CDs in the presence of FITC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optical property of CDs-FITC conjugates.
ce:section.ce:para: In order to confirm the conjugation of CDs with FITC, the FT-IR spectrum of CDs-FITC conjugates was recorded (Fig. S5). The new characteristic absorption bands centered at 1578cm−1 and 1653cm−1 can be ascribed to the aromatic ring skeleton stretching vibration and 3100cm−1 to the aromatic ring CH stretching vibration, thus verifying the existence of FITC. As shown in Fig. S6, the fluorescence spectra of CDs-FITC conjugates (33.0μg/mL) varied with the pH value in 20mM phosphate buffer solution. It can be seen that, with the increase in pH value, the CDs emission spectrum slightly decreased, and the intensity of emission band at 520nm increased, which is similar to the results reported by Du et al. [36]. Furthermore, fluorescence emission spectra of CDs and CDs-FITC conjugates were used to analyze the change of emission spectra of CDs after conjugation with FITC. As shown in Fig. S7, the emission peak of CDs showed a blue shift from 454 to 447nm. In addition, the QY of CDs-FITC conjugates was also measured and calculated to be 5.4%, which was a little less than that of CDs (6.5%).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The occurrence of FRET in CDs-FITC conjugates
ce:section.ce:para: Upon mixing, the emitted light of CDs will be absorbed by FITC, leading to the occurrence of IFE and the decrease of the fluorescence intensity of CDs. However, based on the FRET theory, the spectral overlap between the emission spectrum of CDs and the UV–vis absorption spectrum of FITC (Fig. 3) may result in the occurrence of FRET. To further explore the influence of FITC on CDs, fluorescence lifetime measurements of CDs-FITC conjugates were performed. From Fig. 10 , it can be clearly seen that the fluorescence lifetime of CDs decreased upon conjugation with FITC, implying the existence of FRET between CDs and FITC. Furthermore, the fluorescence lifetime measurements at different pH values were also studied. The results (Table 1) revealed that the variation of pH has no obvious impact on the decrease degree of fluorescence lifetimes. The formation of a thiourea bond between CDs and FITC brought CDs and FITC in proximity, leading to the occurrence of FRET (Fig. S8).

In this study, the effect of FITC on CDs was explored in two different ways. When CDs and FITC were mixed directly, the emitted light of CDs was absorbed by FITC, leading to a fluorescence decrease of CDs due to the IFE between them. However, as CDs were conjugated with FITC, FRET occurred between CDs and FITC. In addition, the IFE efficiency at pH5.1, 7.0 and 9.0 decreased progressively due to the decrease of spectral overlap between the emission spectra of CDs and the UV–vis absorption spectra of FITC, indicating the pH-dependence of IFE. This work reasonably explains two different effects of FITC on CDs. The results about IFE or FRET between CDs and FITC may have potential use in fluorescent sensors or intracellular pH probe, and they also provide some novel insights into the interactions between other nanoparticles and fluorophores.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (31372439, 21205043, 21375043).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2016.08.018.
